reasoning_summary: For live run 20260421_122942_bf16_gemm_v1_3eed315 at 43.250160 ms, the round-7 PTX writer cleanup is not a generic regression to discard. It exposed a materially better performance surface: registers collapsed from 198 to 104, active warps jumped from 16.64% to 33.20%, occupancy_limit_registers moved from 2 to 4, and end-to-end time improved by 2.77 ms. The reason it cannot be trusted yet is correctness, not speed. The direct code evidence is in the PTX export path: the new explicit store sweep only emits TileRow 0 and 1 even though `FixedHotBandTile128x128::kWarpMmaTilesM` is 4, so round 8 should focus first on repairing that writer coverage bug while preserving the new low-register surface. Only after that should the loop spend budget on barrier retiming. A full revert becomes the guardrail fallback, not the main recommendation, because this is exactly the kind of correctness-fail that can hide a real performance step forward.

family_audit.reason: The latest run exposed a potentially high-value 104-register / 33.2%-active-warps surface. The primary job is to make that writer shape correct before considering any rollback.
Once correctness is repaired, the new surface is barrier-heavy at 12.28% and likely worth a bounded seam retime. It is second only because correctness remains the blocking gate.
Reverting to the last correct writer is the safety net if the low-register repair cannot be made correct, but it should not outrank the chance to preserve the new faster surface.
Round 7 already validated that this family can unlock a large occupancy jump; the current task is to make that family correctness-safe rather than abandon it.
The current 128x128 PTX path just showed it can reach a much better register budget, so the loop should harvest that before paying the cost of a broader geometry change.

directions.name: Repair the PTX writer row sweep while preserving the 104-register surface
directions.hypothesis: The new PTX export helper shape is likely directionally correct for performance, but the writer coverage is wrong. `FixedHotBandTile128x128::kWarpMmaTilesM` is 4 while the explicit `ptx_wmma_store_tile_pairs_64x64_ptx_microkernel()` sweep currently emits only TileRow 0 and 1, so half of the warp tile never reaches global memory. Restoring full tile-row coverage or an equivalent correctness-safe row sweep should recover correctness while keeping most of the new 104-register / 33.2%-active-warps surface intact.
directions.expected_bottleneck: correctness recovery on the PTX export/store path first; if preserved, the new steady-state signature becomes barrier-heavy and more memory-active rather than occupancy-limited

score_breakdown.reason: Highest-priority move because it turns a fast but wrong low-register writer into a candidate real win.

ranking_notes: This is a direct code-level correctness bug with an unusually strong performance signature behind it.
Do not revert the whole family before testing whether the missing row sweep is the only blocker.
A correctness-safe 104-register surface would materially change the search frontier.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if repairing writer coverage necessarily restores the old 198-register surface or if correctness still fails after full tile-row emission is restored.

target_hotspots.explanation: The new writer shape cut the kernel to 104 registers per thread, which is worth preserving if correctness can be recovered.
Achieved occupancy doubled, so the repair should protect this new regime.

expected_local_changes: Restore full PTX tile-row emission for TileRow 0..3 or an equivalent correctness-safe sweep.
Keep the new per-tile export helper structure instead of reverting wholesale to the old recursive writer.
Preserve the current shared scratch size and late address arithmetic shape as much as correctness allows.

guardrail_metrics.reason: The repair should not throw away the new four-CTA register budget.
The main opportunity is the new active-warps regime; keep it if at all possible.
This is the blocking failure that must be cleared before treating the speedup as real.

directions.name: After the writer repair, retime the barrier seam on the 104-register anchor
directions.hypothesis: If the writer repair preserves anything close to the new 104-register surface, the bottleneck picture changes immediately: barrier rises to 12.28%, long-scoreboard to 10.4%, and memory throughput to 69.06%. That makes the wait-group / __syncthreads seam the next bounded exploit on top of the repaired low-register anchor.
directions.expected_bottleneck: synchronization_barrier_issue with secondary global_memory_bound behavior on the corrected 104-register PTX surface

score_breakdown.reason: Best follow-on exploit if dir_01 succeeds, because the new faster surface is now barrier-limited rather than register-limited.

ranking_notes: This should not go first because the current low-register surface is still incorrect.
The barrier seam matters more now than it did before because occupancy doubled and memory traffic rose sharply.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if barrier retiming requires giving back the repaired 104-register surface or if barrier improves without any runtime or correctness benefit.

target_hotspots.explanation: Barrier stalls more than doubled on the new low-register surface.
Once occupancy is unlocked, the next seam will be synchronization plus memory movement.

expected_local_changes: Leave the repaired low-register writer in place and retime only the cp.async wait-group / CTA sync seam.
Keep the shared-memory budget fixed at the current 22016-byte level.
Do not reintroduce extra writer temporaries while tuning the handoff.

guardrail_metrics.reason: The barrier follow-up should retain the new occupancy headroom.
Any barrier follow-up only matters on a corrected writer surface.

directions.name: Fallback: restore the last correct PTX writer semantics if the low-register repair collapses
directions.hypothesis: If restoring full tile-row coverage on the new writer shape forces registers back up or keeps failing correctness, the loop still needs a correctness-safe fallback. In that case, recover the last correct PTX writer semantics from b79a9bf while preserving the latest workflow/workload support code on HEAD.
directions.expected_bottleneck: fallback recovery to the last correctness-safe current-workload PTX writer surface

score_breakdown.reason: Necessary fallback if the high-value low-register surface cannot be made correct, but it should not outrank the chance to preserve that new surface.

ranking_notes: This is the guardrail fallback, not the preferred next move.
Use it only if dir_01 cannot recover correctness without collapsing the new occupancy gain.
Keep workflow and workload support files on HEAD even if the implementation surface is restored.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if this restore is selected before trying the direct writer repair, unless new evidence shows the low-register surface cannot be made correct.

target_hotspots.explanation: This restore is only a fallback because the current faster surface is too valuable to drop casually.

expected_local_changes: Reinstate the last correctness-safe PTX writer semantics from b79a9bf if the direct low-register repair fails.
Preserve current workflow/workload support files on HEAD.
Treat this as guardrail recovery, not as a new optimization family.

guardrail_metrics.reason: The whole point of the fallback is to recover correctness quickly.

notes: Round 8/100 diagnosis emitted after a correctness-failing but highly promising 104-register PTX writer surface appeared.
